pattern: Rooting a BFS tree at every vertex and watching for a non-tree edge that links two nodes already at known depths turns 'find the shortest cycle' into a depth-sum computation.
hints: A cycle through a vertex can be found by exploring outward from it layer by layer and noticing when the search meets itself.
Run a BFS from each vertex; whenever an edge connects two nodes that are both already reached in that BFS (and it is not the edge you just walked in on), you have found a cycle candidate.
The candidate cycle length is depth[u] + depth[v] + 1 for the two endpoints of that extra edge; take the minimum such value over every BFS root and every extra edge.

edge_inputs.description: a single triangle among otherwise isolated vertices
a tree only, no cycle exists
smallest possible n with a single edge, no cycle
disconnected graph, one component has a square cycle
two overlapping cycles of different lengths, must return the shorter one
a long cycle plus a chord that creates a much shorter inner cycle
fully isolated vertices, no edges beyond a lone connecting pair, no cycle
self-contained 4-cycle equal in length to a competing 5-cycle sharing a vertex

stress.description: random sparse graph on up to 60 vertices with roughly as many edges as vertices, seeded so cycles may or may not appear
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 60))
    all_pairs = [(u, v) for u in range(n) for v in range(u + 1, n)]
    rng.shuffle(all_pairs)
    m = min(len(all_pairs), n + rng.randrange(0, n // 2 + 1))
    edges = [list(p) for p in all_pairs[:m]]
    return [n, edges]
stress.note: edge count kept close to vertex count so both cyclic and acyclic components show up across seeds
